title: Riven hazel garden rake handle
subtitle: A rived and drawknifed green hazel handle for a garden rake
excerpt: A garden rake handle takes 90 minutes to produce from a green hazel rod: rive a 5 cm hazel section into two staves, drawknife each stave to a 3 cm oval handle on the shaving horse, and leave to season for 4 weeks before fitting. Riving follows the grain so the handle is stronger than a sawn one of the same diameter.
sourceNotes: Traditional British kitchen-garden tool handle construction; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for handle dimensions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for drawknife technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently cut timber, still high in moisture content. Splits and shaves easily under hand tools.
An L-shaped cleaving tool: a blade driven into the end grain with a mallet, then levered to split the wood along the grain.
A long two-handled blade drawn toward the body along a workpiece, removing shavings on the pull stroke. Used on a shaving horse for rapid stock removal.

content.text: A riven handle is stronger than a sawn one because the grain runs unbroken along the full length. A sawn handle cuts across growth rings; a riven stave follows them. The difference matters for a rake handle that flexes under load: a sawn handle eventually fails at the weakest cross-grain point, a riven one does not. This handle starts from a 5 cm diameter section of
green wood
hazel and ends at the shaving horse as a finished 3 cm oval, 150 cm long.
The wood

A freshly felled hazel section: 155 cm long, 5 to 6 cm diameter, straight-grained with no knots in the working length. Coppice hazel is ideal; the rods from a managed stool are straight and even-grained. Ash works equally well and produces a slightly springier handle. Avoid any section with visible spiral grain; a riven stave from spiral-grained wood will twist off-axis as it dries.
Tools
A
froe
and beetle mallet for the riving split. A
drawknife

on a shaving horse for the shaping work. A sloyd knife for the tenon at the rake-head end. The shaving horse must be pegged to the floor or used on a non-slip surface; the drawknife pull is substantial and an unsecured horse will slide.
Method

Mark the centre of the end grain with a pencil cross. Set the froe blade on the end grain at the centre mark, bevelled face toward you. Strike the froe spine with the beetle mallet three or four times to drive the blade 4 to 5 cm into the end. Lever the froe handle forward and back; the split will run down the section following the grain. If the split runs off-centre, lever against the thicker half to steer it back.

Separate the two staves. Each should be a half-round section, 5 to 6 cm across the flat face and 2.5 to 3 cm thick at the centre. Select the straighter of the two for the handle; the second stave makes a second handle or a shaping trial piece.

Secure the stave in the shaving horse. Grip the drawknife with both handles at shoulder width and draw it toward you along the grain, working off the high corners of the half-round first. Reduce the stave to a 3.5 cm square section, then to a 3 cm oval. Work with the grain; if the drawknife digs or chatters, flip the stave end-for-end and work from the other direction.

Shape the rake-head tenon at the lower end: a 2 cm length reduced to 1.8 cm diameter to fit a standard rake socket. Shape the grip end to a slight bulge; thicker at the middle of the palm grip; using the drawknife and a few final sloyd-knife passes. The slight bulge prevents the handle twisting under load.

Leave the handle to season for 4 weeks in a dry place before fitting. The green hazel will shrink as it dries; the tenon will tighten in the socket as moisture leaves. Do not fit the handle while still fully green; the shrinkage will loosen a joint that was fitted wet.
Finishing

Leave unfinished; a bare wood handle gives more grip than an oiled one for most garden work. If the handle is to be stored outdoors, apply one coat of boiled linseed oil to the tenon end only, where soil contact is greatest. The main grip should stay dry for better control.
Variations
Ash: similar riving properties to hazel, slightly more spring in the finished handle. The traditional choice for spade and fork handles.
Shorter hoe handle: 100 cm, same oval cross-section. Rive from a 4 cm diameter section.
Care
Store horizontally in a dry shed. If the tenon joint loosens after a dry summer, soak the tenon end in water overnight; the wood will swell and re-tighten in the socket. Re-oil the tenon annually.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the riven rake handle.

items.cause: Grain not perfectly straight, or froe not centred on the end grain
items.fix: Lever the froe against the thicker half to steer the split back toward the centre. Work in short increments; do not try to force the full length in one pass.

items.symptom: Drawknife digs into the grain and lifts a splinter
items.fix: Flip the stave end-for-end in the shaving horse and cut from the other direction. On straight-grained hazel the grain direction should be consistent, but any twist in the stave requires alternating directions.

items.cause: Spiral grain in the original hazel section
items.fix: A twisted handle cannot be straightened after drying. Avoid sections with spiral grain; check by looking down the end grain for rings that spiral rather than running parallel to the long axis.
